title: SSH Public Key Authentication
description: Passwordless SSH authentication using asymmetric cryptography — more secure than passwords.
fullExplanation: SSH public key authentication uses an asymmetric key pair: a private key (kept secret on the client) and a public key (placed on the server in ~/.ssh/authorized_keys). During authentication, the server sends a challenge encrypted with the client's public key. Only the holder of the private key can decrypt it and prove identity. This is immune to password brute-force attacks and phishing.

process.description: Client generates key pair: ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C 'comment'
Client copies public key to server: ssh-copy-id user@server (appends to ~/.ssh/authorized_keys)
Server sends challenge: random data encrypted with client's public key
Client decrypts challenge with private key, sends back signed response
Server verifies signature — authentication successful, no password needed

keyFacts: Private key never leaves the client — server only has public key
Passphrase: encrypt private key at rest — required if key file is stolen
ssh-agent: caches decrypted private key in memory — type passphrase once per session
Authorized keys: ~/.ssh/authorized_keys on server — one public key per line
Certificate-based auth: SSH CA signs user keys — scales better than individual authorized_keys
Disable password auth: PasswordAuthentication no in /etc/ssh/sshd_config
Ed25519 keys are 68 bytes — much smaller than RSA-4096 (3.3KB)
